source.title: Hippocampal-diencephalic-cingulate networks for memory and emotion: An anatomical guide

confidence_rationale: Comprehensive tract-tracing review of the Papez circuit with detailed topographic mapping of hippocampal-diencephalic-cingulate connectivity. Directly establishes the multiply-interconnected, reciprocal architecture that provides the anatomical substrate for write-profile gating across the circuit. Confidence 0.78 because the review establishes anatomy (what the connections are) rather than functional write-gating directly; the REE translation requires an inferential step from connectivity to gating function.
failure_signatures: The review is primarily anatomical (tract-tracing); functional write-gating claims require additional electrophysiology or lesion evidence.
Most data are from rodent tract-tracing studies; human homology is assumed but not always directly tested.
The review covers general memory functions of the circuit; action-mode specificity (habitual vs deliberate vs simulated) is not directly addressed.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The hippocampal-diencephalic-cingulate network comprises the hippocampus/parahippocampal regions, mammillary bodies, anterior thalamic nuclei, and cingulate gyrus, connected by multiple reciprocal pathways (not a simple serial loop). The circuit is topographically organised into parallel sub-pathways that presumably reflect multiple functional streams. Damage anywhere in the circuit produces episodic memory deficits. The mammillary bodies are an exception: they receive hippocampal output (via fornix) but their projections back to hippocampus are indirect (via anterior thalamus and cingulate).
mapping.ree_translation: For ARC-007: the hippocampal-diencephalic-cingulate network is the anatomical substrate through which trajectory proposals (E3 planning) receive write-enabling signals. The anterior thalamic nuclei, receiving both hippocampal and mammillary input and projecting to cingulate and retrosplenial cortex, are positioned as a convergence node where action-mode context (deliberate vs habitual vs simulated) could gate whether phi(z) updates propagate. For MECH-094: the multiple reciprocal pathways in this circuit include the cingulate-to-hippocampal feedback that is anatomically positioned to implement the hypothesis_tag signal - cingulate cortex (particularly ACC/RSC) projects back to hippocampus via entorhinal cortex and can carry top-down state information that would gate encoding. When this top-down signal is absent or disrupted (tag loss), hippocampal encoding proceeds without the gating that marks content as simulated.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The functional interpretation of this anatomy as a write-gate is inferred from the topology of connections and from lesion data showing encoding failure when circuit nodes are damaged. The paper does not directly test action-mode-specific write-gating. The transition from 'anatomy supports gating' to 'anatomy implements hypothesis_tag' requires additional inferential steps.
mapping.source_context: Review paper integrating rodent and human tract-tracing data, published in Brain and Neuroscience Advances (Cardiff University group, Aggleton lab). Focuses on updating the original Papez serial-loop concept with evidence for parallel, reciprocal pathways.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality: comprehensive, authoritative review from the leading group on anterior thalamic nuclei and hippocampal memory circuits. Mapping fidelity moderate: anatomy is established; functional gating interpretation is inferred. Transfer risk low: human and rodent data both included; episodic memory function of this circuit is well-conserved.
